metadata.name: KnowledgeBot Custom Knowledge Base
metadata.description: Local facts the bot knows without searching the web. Add your own entries here.

entries.content: KnowledgeBot is a conversational AI assistant built with LangChain, Groq (LLaMA 3.1), DuckDuckGo, Wikipedia, and Streamlit. It remembers conversations, searches the web in real time, and can answer from a custom knowledge base. It was built as a production-level learning project across 9 milestones.

KnowledgeBot uses: Python 3.10+, LangChain 0.3.x for orchestration, Groq with LLaMA 3.1-8b-instant for LLM inference, DuckDuckGo for web search, Wikipedia API for encyclopedic lookups, Streamlit for the chat UI, and python-dotenv for environment management.

LangChain is an open-source framework for building applications powered by large language models. It provides abstractions for chains, agents, memory, tools, and retrievers. Key components: ChatPromptTemplate for prompt management, AgentExecutor for tool-calling loops, RunnableWithMessageHistory for memory, and LCEL (LangChain Expression Language) for composing pipelines with the | operator.

Groq is an AI infrastructure company that provides ultra-fast LLM inference using its custom Language Processing Unit (LPU) chip. It offers a free tier API supporting models like LLaMA 3.1, Mixtral, and Gemma. Groq is known for producing responses 10-20x faster than GPU-based inference. API keys are free at console.groq.com.

The ReAct (Reason + Act) pattern is how KnowledgeBot's agent works. For each user query: (1) The LLM REASONS about what information it needs. (2) It ACTS by calling a tool (web_search or wikipedia). (3) It OBSERVES the tool output. (4) It repeats until it has enough to answer, then returns a final response. This loop is managed by AgentExecutor with a max of 5 iterations.

KnowledgeBot supports two memory types: (1) ConversationBufferMemory — stores every message verbatim. Simple but grows unboundedly. Best for short sessions. (2) ConversationSummaryMemory — uses the LLM to summarise older turns while keeping recent ones in full. Prevents context overflow. Best for long sessions. Both use session_id for isolation between different users or browser tabs.

To set up the project: (1) python -m venv venv. (2) Activate: venv\Scripts\activate (Windows) or source venv/bin/activate (Mac/Linux). (3) pip install -r requirements.txt. (4) Copy .env.example to .env and add your GROQ_API_KEY. (5) Run: streamlit run run.py from the project root.

Streamlit reruns the entire script from top to bottom on every user interaction. session_state is the persistence layer that survives reruns. KnowledgeBot stores messages and query counts in session_state for the UI, and uses a separate LangChain ChatMessageHistory for the LLM context. @st.cache_resource caches the agent object so it is built only once.

KnowledgeBot uses only free APIs: (1) Groq — free tier with ~14,400 tokens/minute. No credit card needed. (2) DuckDuckGo Search — completely free, no API key required. (3) Wikipedia API — completely free, no API key required. The only required key is GROQ_API_KEY, available free at console.groq.com.

KnowledgeBot was built across 9 milestones: M1 (Project Setup + LLM test), M2 (Stateless CLI chatbot), M3 (Memory-enabled chatbot), M4 (Tool-enabled agent), M5 (Memory + Tools combined), M6 (Streamlit UI), M7 (Modular refactor), M8 (README + GitHub), M9 (Summary memory + Knowledge base + Prompt engineering).
The full answer the bot should give when asked about this topic.
